title: The Bridge
tagline: He was told it was impossible. He built it anyway. When a storm destroyed it, he had the sea whipped.
hook_line: He needed to cross the Hellespont. His engineers said a bridge of boats was impossible. He built two of them.

scenes.title: The Problem
scenes.narration: Between Persia and Greece lay the Hellespont — one mile of sea. For 2.5 million soldiers, that mile was everything.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, the Hellespont strait from above — dark churning water between two landmasses, Persian army visible on the eastern shore extending to the horizon, Greece a thin line on the western horizon, the water between them the only obstacle, dramatic aerial, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: The Order
scenes.narration: Xerxes ordered two bridges of boats — pontoon bridges stretching a full mile across open sea. His engineers had never built anything like it. They built it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, engineers and soldiers working at impossible scale to lash hundreds of boats together across the Hellespont, creating two parallel pontoon bridges a mile long, the engineering achievement extraordinary, torchlight on dark water, dramatic construction scene, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: The Storm
scenes.narration: Then a storm destroyed both bridges. The work of months. Gone in one night. Xerxes's response is one of the most remembered moments in ancient history.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, the completed pontoon bridges being torn apart by a violent Hellespont storm, waves crashing over the boat bridge, cables snapping, the night sky violent with lightning, the engineering destroyed in one catastrophic night, dramatic destruction, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: He Had the Sea Whipped
scenes.narration: He ordered the Hellespont whipped. Three hundred lashes. Chains thrown into the water. The sea was to be punished for its insubordination.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Persian soldiers at the water's edge delivering three hundred lashes to the Hellespont on Xerxes's command, chains being cast into the waves, the act simultaneously absurd and terrifying in its absolute assertion of will, soldiers obeying without question, dramatic, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: What This Means
scenes.narration: Modern historians debate whether this actually happened. But every soldier in his army believed it. That was what mattered. His will extended to the sea itself.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Persian soldiers watching the sea-whipping in silence — some with expressions of awe, some with fear — the collective belief of 2.5 million men that their king's will had no limit, Xerxes watching from a throne carried to the shore, composure absolute, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: He Built Them Again
scenes.narration: Then he built both bridges again. The engineers who had built the first ones and failed to make them storm-proof were executed. The new engineers did not make the same mistake.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, new construction beginning immediately on the Hellespont, engineers working at frantic precision, heavier cables, better anchor systems, the second attempt built to withstand anything, urgency and fear driving the construction, dramatic torchlight, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: Seven Days
scenes.narration: It took the army seven days and seven nights to cross. Continuous. Unbroken. A column of 2.5 million soldiers crossing a one-mile bridge of boats.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, the completed Hellespont bridge from above showing the continuous flow of Persian forces crossing — infantry, cavalry, war elephants, supply wagons — the crossing unbroken day and night, torches on the bridge at night, sunlight on armor by day, the scale incomprehensible, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: Xerxes Watches
scenes.narration: Xerxes watched from a marble throne placed at the crossing point. Herodotus wrote that he wept. When asked why, he said: 'I was overcome with pity, when I thought how short the whole of human life is.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Xerxes seated on a marble throne at the bridge head watching his army cross, tears visible on his face, surrounded by his generals who are confused by the tears, the army crossing in the background endless and unbroken, a king confronting mortality at the moment of his greatest power, dramatic, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: What the Greeks Saw
scenes.narration: Greek scouts reported the crossing. Their generals didn't believe them. No army that size existed. They went to look themselves. They came back and said nothing for a long time.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Greek scouts on a distant hillside looking through the morning haze at the Hellespont crossing — tiny figures against the incomprehensible scale of the Persian army in motion, their expressions moving from disbelief to silence, the crushing weight of what they're witnessing, dramatic wide, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: He Was Already in Greece
scenes.narration: By the time the Greek city-states finished debating whether to fight, Xerxes was already in Greece. The bridge had solved the problem. His patience had solved everything else.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Xerxes at the head of his army on European soil for the first time, the Hellespont visible behind them fully crossed, Greece ahead, the army stretching behind him to the horizon, Xerxes on horseback at the head of the column looking forward — not back — toward Greece, dramatic, 1080x1920 vertical

viral_hook: His bridge was destroyed by a storm. He had the sea whipped 300 times. Then he built the bridge again.
highlight_note: The sea-whipping scene — simultaneously absurd and terrifying — is the defining Xerxes moment. His will had no limit. His army believed this. That belief was a weapon.
rewatch_bait: Scene 8 — Xerxes weeping as his army crosses. The most powerful man in the world, crying at the sight of his own power. Why? Rewatch to hear the quote.
youtube_title: His Bridge Was Destroyed. He Had the Sea Whipped 300 Times. Then He Built Another Bridge. | Gods & Glory
lesson: Will without limit is terrifying not because it always works — but because it never stops.
arc_notes: EP002 establishes the logistical miracle of the crossing and the psychological dimension of Xerxes — his will, his tears, his composure. Cross-feeds ML: ELDER-7's line 'he has had 2,800 years of practice' lands differently after this episode.
